one_line_takeaway: A per-client teacher/student federated IDS with adaptive bidirectional distillation, feature alignment, and class-balanced loss that stays robust to extreme non-IID IoT traffic (78.22% F1 on IoTID20 alpha=0.1 vs 61.29% next-best) while transmitting only a compact student model.
key_finding: Under extreme data heterogeneity (Dirichlet alpha=0.1) on IoTID20, all Sentinel variants dominate: Sentinel-III reaches 78.22% macro F1 (Sentinel-II 78.13%, Sentinel-I 77.92%), vs the next-best personalized baseline FedRep at 61.29% (~17-point margin), while FedAvg and Ditto collapse to 13.07%, Scaffold to 11.48%, and FedProx to 31.36%. On 5GNIDD at alpha=0.1, Sentinel-II attains 97.29% macro F1 vs APPLE 88.90% and Scaffold 88.78%. Sentinel also gives markedly lower variance (Sentinel-II 5GNIDD alpha=0.1 F1 std +/-5.08% vs Ditto +/-23.69% and FedKD +/-25.73%) and better minority-class detection (IID IoTID20 macro recall 93.47% vs APPLE 89.41%). The ablation shows the three mechanisms are synergistic: full Sentinel-I hits 77.92% F1 at alpha=0.1, and removing only the class-balanced loss drops it sharply to 71.19%. Communication stays cheap — only the 7,813-parameter student is transmitted (teacher is 20,741 params) — at the cost of higher per-round compute (~297s Sentinel-I, ~325s Sentinel-II, ~550s Sentinel-III on IoTID20).
core_contribution: Sentinel is a personalized federated intrusion detection (pFed-IDS) framework for heterogeneous IoT networks. Its central design is a per-client dual-model architecture: a larger personalized teacher model that stays local to capture the client's own (non-IID) traffic distribution, and a lightweight shared student model that is the only thing transmitted to/from the server, cutting communication cost. The paper's own contributions (distinct from cited work it builds on) are: (1) an adaptive bidirectional knowledge-distillation loss whose teacher-to-student term is weighted by (1-agreement) and student-to-teacher term by joint confidence, with a temperature that anneals from 3.0 to 1.0 across rounds; (2) a multi-component feature-alignment loss combining geometric MSE on nearest-neighbor-matched features, directional (cosine) alignment, and a memory-bank InfoNCE contrastive term to bridge heterogeneous teacher/student embedding spaces; (3) an EMA-based dynamic loss-weighting scheme that self-tunes the KD and alignment weights each batch from real-time agreement/alignment scores. It builds on but does not claim: the dual-model KD idea (FedKD [26]), class-balanced 'effective number of samples' loss (Cui et al. [49]), nearest-node feature matching (FedDNA [51]), InfoNCE (van den Oord [52]), and the server's normalized-gradient/Byzantine-robust aggregation (FedNGA, Zuo et al. [44]), which Sentinel adopts with equal client weighting and server momentum.
threat_model: Detecting network intrusions / cyberattacks (including class-imbalanced, rare 'minority-class' attacks and zero-day-style threats) in heterogeneous IoT traffic, where an IDS on each edge/MEC gateway classifies benign vs malicious flows. The federated setting additionally aims to preserve client data privacy (raw traffic never leaves the device; only the student model is shared) and, by design, to resist Byzantine/poisoning attacks from malicious clients submitting abnormal gradients via normalized-gradient aggregation — though this poisoning-robustness claim is asserted from the aggregation design, not empirically evaluated in the paper.
limitations: Byzantine/poisoning robustness is a design claim inherited from FedNGA aggregation but is never tested — no adversarial/poisoning experiments are run, and dropout/straggler filtering is explicitly deactivated (pdrop=0, Tthresh=10,000s). Evaluation is limited to 2 datasets and a small controlled cross-silo setup of exactly 10 fully-participating clients (rho=1), leaving large-scale cross-device behavior with partial participation untested. Sentinel incurs higher per-round computation than lightweight baselines like FedAvg (e.g., ~325s vs 76s/round on IoTID20), justified only by claimed faster convergence. Privacy is only partial: the personalized teacher (used for final inference) never leaves the client, so global consensus rests on the student; gradient-inversion resistance is asserted, not measured. Sentinel-III is noted as not directly comparable to the DNN-only baselines. No statistical significance testing beyond reported mean+/-std across the 10 clients.

quantitative_results: IoTID20 alpha=0.1 macro F1: Sentinel-III 78.22%, Sentinel-II 78.13%, Sentinel-I 77.92%; next-best FedRep 61.29%; FedAvg & Ditto 13.07%, Scaffold 11.48%, FedProx 31.36%
5GNIDD alpha=0.1 macro F1: Sentinel-II 97.29%, Sentinel-I 96.95%, Sentinel-III 96.20%; vs APPLE 88.90%, Scaffold 88.78%
IoTID20 alpha=1 macro F1: Sentinel-II 88.78% vs FedRep 74.56%
IID IoTID20 macro recall: Sentinel-II 93.47% vs APPLE 89.41%, BalanceFL 84.45%
Ablation (IoTID20, Sentinel-I, alpha=0.1): full model 77.92% F1; removing class-balanced loss -> 71.19%; CE+Bi-KD only 69.72%; CB+Bi-KD 71.98%
Variance at 5GNIDD alpha=0.1 F1 std: Sentinel-II +/-5.08% vs Ditto +/-23.69%, FedKD +/-25.73%
Model sizes: teacher 20,741 params, student 7,813 params (only student transmitted)
Per-round time on IoTID20: Sentinel-I ~297s, Sentinel-II ~325s, Sentinel-III ~550s (vs FedAvg 76s)
Hyperparameters: beta=0.999 (CB loss), Tbase=3.0->Tmin=1.0, contrastive tau=0.1, EMA alpha decays 0.9->0.7, server LR eta=1.0, momentum beta=0.9
